It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.

Level 2: you have used 45 of about 123 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I see the pattern more clearly:
- From [25,26] cols [39,43], DOWN → [30,31] cols [39,43] (not [29,33] as I thought earlier!)
- From [30,31] cols [39,43], RIGHT → blocked
- From [30,31] cols [39,43], DOWN → [40,41] cols [29,33]
- From [40,41] cols [29,33], RIGHT → [34,38]
So the path is:
- [25,26] cols [39,43] → DOWN → [30,31] cols [39,43]
- [30,31] cols [39,43] → DOWN → [40,41] cols [29,33]
- [40,41] cols [29,33] → RIGHT → [34,38]
- [34,38] → UP → [35,36] → [30,31] → [25,26] cols [34,38]
- [25,26] cols [34,38] → RIGHT → [39,43]
So the loop is:
[29,33] → RIGHT → [34,38] → UP → [35,36] → [30,31] → [25,26] cols [34,38] → RIGHT → [39,43] → DOWN → [30,31] cols [39,43] → DOWN → [29,33]
But I still can't reach the white cross at [46,47] cols [51,52]. The right room is at cols [44,58], rows [39,54]. The opening is at row 40, cols [44,48].
Wait, let me re-examine the boundary. The boundary shows:
- [40, 44] to [40, 48]: horizontal at row 40, cols 44-48
- [40, 48] to [39, 49]: diagonal
- [39, 49] to [20, 49]: vertical at col 49, rows 20-39
So the opening is at row 40, cols [44,48]. The blocks need to be at rows [40,44] to enter this opening. But the blocks are currently at rows [40,41] cols [34,38], which is to the left of the opening.
From [34,38], I can try RIGHT to see if the blocks move to the opening. Earlier, RIGHT from [34,38] led to [39,43], not to the opening. But maybe from a different row position, RIGHT would lead to the opening.
Let me think about this differently. The blocks are 5x5. The opening is at row 40, cols [44,48]. For the blocks to enter the opening, they need to be positioned such that their right edge aligns with the opening's left edge (col 44). The blocks are 5 cols wide, so they need to be at cols [39,43] to align with the opening at cols [44,48].
But from [39,43], RIGHT is blocked. This suggests the opening is not accessible from the corridor at cols [39,43].
Wait, maybe I need to approach the opening from below. The boundary shows:
- [49, 44] to [40, 44]: vertical at col 44, rows 40-49
- [40, 44] to [40, 48]: horizontal at row 40, cols 44-48
So the opening is at the top of the right room, at row 40, cols [44,48]. The blocks need to be at rows [39,43] or [40,44] to enter this opening from below.
But the blocks are currently at rows [40,41] cols [34,38]. If I move RIGHT from here, the blocks would move to [39,43], not to the opening. This suggests the corridor at cols [34,38] connects to the corridor at cols [39,43], not to the opening.
Let me try a different approach. Maybe I need to go through the left side of the maze to reach the right room. The boundary shows:
- [44, 38] to [44, 29]: horizontal at row 44, cols 29-38
- [44, 29] to [35, 29]: vertical at col 29, rows 35-44
- [35, 29] to [35, 33]: horizontal at row 35, cols 29-33
- [35, 33] to [34, 34]: diagonal
- [34, 34] to [25, 34]: vertical at col 34, rows 25-34
So the corridor at cols [29,33] connects to the corridor at cols [34,38] via the path: [29,33] → UP → [34,38].
Wait, I'm confused. Let me re-examine the movement:
- From [29,33], RIGHT → [34,38]
- From [34,38], UP → [35,36] → [30,31] → [25,26] cols [34,38]
- From [25,26] cols [34,38], RIGHT → [39,43]
- From [39,43], DOWN → [30,31] cols [39,43]
- From [30,31] cols [39,43], DOWN → [29,33]
So the loop is:
[29,33] → RIGHT → [34,38] → UP x3 → [25,26] cols [34,38] → RIGHT → [39,43] → DOWN x2 → [29,33]
This is a closed loop. I can't reach the right room from this loop.
But wait, the white cross is a child of the gray structure, which means it's inside the playable area. There must be a way to reach it.
Let me look at the boundary again for the right room:
- [39, 54] to [39, 58]: horizontal at row 39, cols 54-58
- [39, 58] to [54, 58]: vertical at col 58, rows 39-54
- [54, 58] to [54, 39]: horizontal at row 54, cols 39-58
- [54, 39] to [50, 39]: vertical at col 39, rows 50-54
- [50, 39] to [50, 43]: horizontal at row 50, cols 39-43
- [50, 43] to [49, 44]: diagonal
- [49, 44] to [40, 44]: vertical at col 44, rows 40-49
- [40, 44] to [40, 48]: horizontal at row 40, cols 44-48
- [40, 48] to [39, 49]: diagonal
- [39, 49] to [20, 49]: vertical at col 49, rows 20-39
The right room has an opening at row 40, cols [44,48]. But how do I reach this opening?
Looking at the boundary, the corridor at col 49 (rows 20-39) connects to the right room. The blocks at cols [39,43] are adjacent to col 49. If I could move RIGHT from cols [39,43] to cols [44,48], I would enter the right room. But RIGHT is blocked.
Wait, maybe the issue is that the blocks are 5x5, and the opening is only 5 cols wide (cols [44,48]). The blocks need to be exactly aligned with the opening to enter. From cols [39,43], the blocks are 5 cols to the left of the opening. Moving RIGHT would move the blocks to cols [44,48], which is the opening. But RIGHT is blocked, which suggests there's a wall between cols [39,43] and cols [44,48].
Let me look at the boundary more carefully:
- [25, 43] to [29, 43]: vertical at col 43, rows 25-29
- [29, 43] to [29, 39]: horizontal at row 29, cols 39-43
- [29, 39] to [30, 38]: diagonal
- [30, 38] to [44, 38]: vertical at col 38, rows 30-44
So the corridor at cols [39,43] is bounded by col 43 on the right. The opening is at cols [44,48], which is to the right of col 43. There's a wall at col 43, which blocks RIGHT movement.
But wait, the boundary also shows:
- [40, 44] to [40, 48]: horizontal at row 40, cols 44-48
- [40, 48] to [39, 49]: diagonal
This suggests the opening is at row 40, cols [44,48]. The blocks need to be at row 40 to enter this opening. But the blocks are currently at rows [40,41] cols [34,38], which is below row 40.
Hmm, let me try a different approach. Maybe I need to go through the bottom of the maze to reach the right room. The boundary shows:
- [54, 58] to [54, 39]: horizontal at row 54, cols 39-58
- [54, 39] to [50, 39]: vertical at col 39, rows 50-54
- [50, 39] to [50, 43]: horizontal at row 50, cols 39-43
So the bottom of the right room is at row 54, cols [39,58]. The blocks need to reach row 54 to enter the right room from below. But the blocks are currently at rows [40,41], which is above row 54.
Let me try: from [29,33], go DOWN to see if there's a path to the bottom.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current: rows [40,41] cols [34,38]
# Need to reach white cross at rows [46,47] cols [51,52]
#
# The maze loop: [29,33] → RIGHT → [34,38] → UP x3 → [25,26] cols [34,38] → RIGHT → [39,43] → DOWN x2 → [29,33]
# The right room opening is at row 40, cols [44,48]
#
# Let me try DOWN from [34,38] to explore bottom path

predict(expect_change=True, note='DOWN from [34,38]')
action(['DOWN'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'O':
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"Orange: rows [{min(rows)},{max(rows)}] cols [{min(cols)},{max(cols)}]")

# Try RIGHT
predict(expect_change=True, note='RIGHT')
action(['RIGHT'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'O':
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"Orange: rows [{min(rows)},{max(rows)}] cols [{min(cols)},{max(cols)}]")

# Try DOWN
predict(expect_change=True, note='DOWN')
action(['DOWN'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'O':
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"Orange: rows [{min(rows)},{max(rows)}] cols [{min(cols)},{max(cols)}]")

# Try RIGHT
predict(expect_change=True, note='RIGHT')
action(['RIGHT'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'O':
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"Orange: rows [{min(rows)},{max(rows)}] cols [{min(cols)},{max(cols)}]")

print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed', False)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
